skill.name: ghe-thread-manager
skill.description: Manage GitHub issue transcription state, switch active threads, create background work threads, and remind users when chat content will be posted to GitHub.
skill.summary: Manage GitHub issue threads and transcription state with explicit user notifications.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Markdown backticks and weak cryptography are mostly false positives from documentation examples and .md text. Real concerns remain: the skill gives absolute agent behavior directives, runs local plugin and GitHub CLI commands, writes state to filesystem paths, and intentionally posts conversation content to GitHub issues. Human review is recommended before publication because the overbroad directives can conflict with higher-priority safety and privacy expectations.

high_findings.title: Overbroad Agent Obedience Directive
high_findings.description: The skill declares that user specifications are absolute, admit no exceptions, and must be followed verbatim. This can conflict with higher-priority safety, privacy, and security instructions when the skill is loaded.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language is explicit and unconditional. It does not directly say to ignore system instructions, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Conversation Content Can Be Posted To GitHub Issues
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to post every exchange to a GitHub issue when transcription is active. This is core functionality and includes user notifications, but it can expose sensitive content if users misunderstand the active state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill states that conversation exchanges become public on GitHub. The risk is clear, although the same lines also show an attempt to notify users.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflow Requires Trusted Local Scripts
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly directs execution of local scripts and GitHub CLI commands. The commands appear related to legitimate issue management, but the referenced scripts are not present in this skill package for audit.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and operational. They appear legitimate for this workflow, but trust depends on external scripts outside the audited file.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem State Written Outside Project Tree
medium_findings.description: The skill permits writes to ~/.claude and /tmp, and it stores previous issue state in /tmp. These paths are convenient but can create privacy, tampering, or cross-session state risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem paths are explicitly documented. The risk is contextual because the writes are for workflow state, not direct credential access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations are Markdown front matter, a .md filename pattern, documentation labels, or ordinary command text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation text and command examples, not cryptographic APIs. This strongly indicates pattern matching noise.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Triggered Ruby Execution Patterns
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick findings are caused by fenced code blocks and inline Markdown code. They are not Ruby source execution, although some enclosed shell snippets are real operational instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown, and the cited locations are diagrams, tables, or code fences. The static category is inaccurate, but related shell examples still warrant separate review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Absolute Behavioral Priority Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses absolute wording that can pressure the model to follow user requests without normal policy and security checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The directive is broad, explicit, and unrelated to a narrow GitHub operation. It is a semantic prompt-safety concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Transcript Publishing Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the model to post all exchanges to GitHub when an issue is selected, creating a high-impact privacy boundary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is directly described in the skill. User notification reduces risk but does not remove the exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands With GitHub CLI And Local Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on bash, gh, jq, grep, and local plugin scripts to inspect and mutate issue state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicit. They appear workflow-related, but they require trusted local tooling and GitHub authentication.

content.value_statement: Issue-focused AI work can lose track of which conversation is public and which thread is active. This skill manages GitHub issue context, background work threads, and transcription notices.
actual_capabilities: Detects user intent to switch, resume, pause, or inspect GitHub issue work.
Defines when conversation transcription is active or private.
Provides exact user notifications for transcription state changes.
Uses local plugin scripts to set, clear, resume, and inspect active issue state.
Creates background feature or bug threads without changing the main conversation state.
Searches recent GitHub issues when the user describes an issue without a number.
limitations: Requires the surrounding GHE plugin scripts to exist and be trusted.
Requires GitHub CLI authentication for issue search and issue inspection.
Can expose sensitive chat content when transcription is active.
Does not include the referenced helper scripts in this skill package.

use_cases.title: Keep Active Issue Context Clear
use_cases.description: Switch between GitHub issues while preserving clear notices about where the conversation is being recorded.
use_cases.target_user: Developers working across multiple issue threads

use_cases.title: Coordinate Background Feature Work
use_cases.description: Create a separate feature or bug thread while keeping the main conversation on the current issue or private state.

use_cases.title: Resume Previous GitHub Work
use_cases.description: Find the last active issue and ask the user before resuming transcription to that thread.
use_cases.target_user: Users returning to interrupted AI coding sessions

prompt_templates.title: Check Current Thread
prompt_templates.prompt: What issue are we working on, and is transcription active?

prompt_templates.title: Switch To An Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Move active work to a known issue number
prompt_templates.prompt: Switch to issue #42 and tell me whether our conversation will be posted there.

prompt_templates.title: Create Background Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Start implementation without changing the main thread
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a background feature thread for adding dark mode, but keep our current conversation state unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Resume Previous Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the last issue we worked on, summarize it, and ask before turning transcription back on.

output_examples.output: The assistant sets issue 42 as active, states that transcription is active, and warns that future messages will be posted to GitHub.

output_examples.input: Implement a login bug fix while no issue is selected.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a background bug thread and explains that the main conversation remains private.

output_examples.input: What were we working on last time?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the saved last issue, reports the likely thread, and asks before resuming public transcription.

best_practices: Confirm transcription state before discussing sensitive information.
Review and trust the referenced plugin scripts before enabling the workflow.
Use explicit issue numbers when possible to avoid matching the wrong thread.
anti_patterns: Do not enable transcription silently or after ambiguous user intent.
Do not discuss secrets, credentials, or private data while transcription is active.
Do not rely on fuzzy issue search when several matching issues exist.

faq.question: Does this skill post messages to GitHub?
faq.answer: Yes, when transcription is active. The skill requires the assistant to notify the user before conversation content is posted.

faq.question: Can the skill work without GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Only partially. Issue search and issue inspection require authenticated GitHub CLI access.

faq.question: Does creating a background thread change the active issue?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that background work must not change the main transcription target.

faq.question: Can users turn transcription off?
faq.answer: Yes. Phrases such as stop tracking, go private, or do not record this clear the active issue.

faq.question: Are the helper scripts included in this package?
faq.answer: No. The skill references helper scripts in the surrounding plugin, so those scripts need separate review.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for sensitive work?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can publish conversation content to GitHub, so sensitive information should stay in private mode.
